findings.title: oracle_runner / panel.score failure after the Solver discards the entire attempt with no trace and no diagnosable cause
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: A long/expensive Solver run completes, then the out-of-band grading host is unreachable (network blip, host restart) OR a single Ollama judge daemon errors — the raw exception propagates straight out of run_attempt. The Solver's completed work is lost (no AttemptState returned), and NO eval_log / trace is written (writer.to_eval_log is only called at line 402, after grading), so the operator gets a bare traceback with no record of the model output that was produced, no terminated_by, and no indication WHICH stage failed. In a pass^k run (run_pass_hat_k) the first sibling's grading failure aborts all k, discarding every completed sibling.
findings.description: The Solver turn is wrapped in try/finally (lines 309-331) so a Solver-side failure is caught and stamped onto terminated_by with an error event. But the ENTIRE post-Solver pipeline runs with no failure handling: `outcome = await oracle_runner(attempt, meta)` (356), `panel_score = await active_panel.score(attempt)` (367), `eval_log = writer.to_eval_log(attempt)` (402), and `event_store.append(eval_log)` (404-406). The grading host and the judge panel are exactly the dependencies most likely to fail at runtime (separate host per §10.4; Ollama daemon per §8.6) — yet a failure there is unrecoverable and produces no partial result and no legible diagnosis. The Stage-A test suite never exercises an oracle_runner or panel that raises (grep over tests confirms every oracle_runner_for returns a canned outcome). This is the gap between 'code that works' (all fakes succeed) and 'code that respects an expensive run' (degrade with a partial, traced result naming the failing stage).
findings.evidence:
outcome = await oracle_runner(attempt, meta)

    panel_score: Score | None = None
    active_panel = panel
    if active_panel is None and judges:
        active_panel = JudgePanel(judges, reducer=panel_reducer, generator_family=generator_family)
    if active_panel is not None:
        panel_score = await active_panel.score(attempt)
    ...
    attempt.scores["oracle"] = grade(attempt, meta, outcome)
    ...
    eval_log = writer.to_eval_log(attempt)
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Wrap the grading + panel block in try/except. On a grading/panel failure: stamp attempt.terminated_by = TerminatedBy.ERROR (or a new GRADING_FAILED), set attempt.error to a structured code/message/hint naming the failing stage ('oracle_runner unreachable' vs 'panel scoring failed'), append an error TraceEvent, still render and (best-effort) persist the eval_log so the Solver transcript survives, and return the populated attempt rather than raising. In run_pass_hat_k, isolate per-sibling failure so one bad sibling does not discard the cohort. Add RED tests: an oracle_runner that raises and a judge that raises, asserting a traced, returned, degraded attempt.

findings.title: A single failing judge aborts the whole panel — no partial-panel degradation
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: In a real Phase-2 cross-family panel (Qwen + Mistral + Command-R via Ollama), if ONE of three judges times out or the daemon hiccups, asyncio.gather (no return_exceptions) propagates that one judge's exception and the panel returns nothing — so a 2-of-3 panel that could still have ruled (quorum-adjacent) instead fails entirely, and per kernel-runtime-001 that failure currently takes down the whole attempt. The verifier layer is the one most exposed to flaky local-model infra, and it has the least tolerance for partial failure.
findings.description: JudgePanel.score fans out to all eligible judges with `asyncio.gather(*(judge(attempt) for judge in eligible))` and no `return_exceptions=True`. Any single judge raising (timeout, connection reset, malformed model output) cancels the gather and propagates. There is no path to 'N-1 judges ruled, here is the partial panel verdict with a note that judge X errored.' The PoLL design (≥3 cross-family) is explicitly about robustness through redundancy, but the implementation makes the panel only as reliable as its least-reliable member. The empty-panel ValueError (411) is legible, but a mid-flight judge failure is not handled at all.
findings.evidence:
# One observable fan-out; judges are independent so we gather concurrently.
        scores = await asyncio.gather(*(judge(attempt) for judge in eligible))

        panel = reduce_scores(list(scores), self.reducer)
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Use `asyncio.gather(..., return_exceptions=True)`, partition results into successes and failures, reduce over the successes, and record the failed judges (id + error) in panel.metadata (e.g. 'judges_errored') so a degraded panel is distinguishable from a clean one. Define a floor (e.g. raise the existing empty-panel ValueError only if ZERO judges succeeded, or below a configurable quorum) so the panel degrades gracefully instead of all-or-nothing. Add a RED test with one raising judge asserting the panel still rules on the survivors and surfaces the failure.

findings.title: Unbounded reads of meta.json / prompt — no size guard at the puzzle trust boundary
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: A puzzle directory (an external/authored artifact — the §1 trust boundary) containing a multi-gigabyte prompt file or meta.json reads the whole file into memory via read_text with no cap, OOMing the kernel process before any budget or sandbox limit applies. The sandbox carefully caps subprocess output at 1 MiB (DEFAULT_MAX_OUTPUT_BYTES) but the puzzle loader — the FIRST place untrusted-author content enters — has no equivalent ceiling.
findings.description: load_puzzle is the defensive front door for puzzle artifacts (the docstring lists missing files, bad JSON, schema failures). It validates structure but never bounds size: `json.loads(meta_path.read_text(...))` (107) and `prompt_path.read_text(...)` (146) slurp arbitrarily large files. The module already models a structured-error discipline (_fail with code/message/hint) and an oracle-key rejection, so a size guard fits naturally. This is a gap in defensive coding at the input trust boundary, contrasting with the deliberate output cap in sandbox.py.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Before reading, stat() each file and reject oversized inputs with a structured PuzzleLoadError (e.g. 'INPUT_META_TOO_LARGE' / 'INPUT_PROMPT_TOO_LARGE') carrying the size and the cap in the hint. Pick sane caps (e.g. meta.json ~1 MiB, prompt ~a few MiB) and make them overridable. Add a RED test planting an oversized prompt and asserting the structured rejection.

findings.title: Budget far smaller than the hard-kill window can never reach HARD_KILL — a tight-budget loop is mislabeled BUDGET
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: A puzzle with tool_call_budget=1 (a valid FILE_INSPECTION value) and the default hard_kill_consecutive_identical=3 can never fire HARD_KILL: the 2nd identical call already exceeds the 1-call budget and raises BUDGET first (hard-kill needs 3 consecutive, requiring ≥2 admitted calls). A genuinely pathological looping Solver on a tiny-budget puzzle is recorded as terminated_by=BUDGET rather than HARD_KILL, so the operator's loop-detection diagnostic silently never triggers for tight budgets — the run terminates correctly but the WHY is misattributed in the trace.
findings.description: record_tool_call checks the loop streak (≥ threshold) before the budget increment, but the streak can only reach the threshold if the budget admits that many calls. When tool_call_budget < hard_kill_threshold - 1, the budget gate always fires first. This is not a correctness bug (the attempt still halts and is non-COMPLETED), but it is an observability/diagnosability gap: terminated_by is the operator's primary signal for WHY an attempt ended, and a loop on a small budget is indistinguishable from honest exhaustion. No guard or note exists for the budget < hard-kill-window relationship, and no test covers it.
findings.evidence:
if self._consecutive_identical >= self._hard_kill_threshold:
            raise BudgetExceeded(
                TerminatedBy.HARD_KILL, ...)

        # 2. Tool-call budget (ANDON: refuse the call that would overrun).
        if self._budget.tool_calls_used + 1 > self._budget.tool_call_budget:
            raise BudgetExceeded(
                TerminatedBy.BUDGET, ...)
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Either document the precedence explicitly (a note that BUDGET subsumes HARD_KILL when budget < threshold-1), or, for better diagnosability, when refusing the over-budget call check whether the refused call would also have continued an identical streak and annotate the BudgetExceeded message / a trace field ('looped_at_exhaustion=True') so a tight-budget loop is still legible. Optionally warn at governor construction when tool_call_budget is too small for hard-kill to ever fire.

findings.title: TraceWriter.to_eval_log emits an ad-hoc Inspect-EvalLog shape with no version tag — future Inspect/consumer drift is silent
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: The eval_log dict is hand-shaped to match 'Inspect EvalLog v2' (docstring) but carries no version/schema marker in the payload. If a future Inspect release or a downstream consumer changes the expected envelope (renames a top-level key, expects a version field), the mismatch is silent: a consumer either misparses or drops fields with no error, and persisted JSONL provenance records can't be told apart by schema version after the fact. inspect-ai is pinned >=0.3 (open upper bound), so the surface this mirrors can move.
findings.description: The module deliberately emits a plain dict rather than an Inspect pydantic type 'so it neither pins nor breaks against Inspect's evolving API' — a reasonable decoupling choice. But the emitted envelope has no self-describing version key (no 'version' / 'schema' / 'eval_log_version'). The shape is committed to in code and tests assert specific keys (samples[0].id, results.terminated_by), so a future shape change has a test signal internally, but a CONSUMER reading a persisted log (attestation JSONL) has nothing to branch on. This is a forward-compat hygiene gap, not a present defect.
findings.evidence:
return {
            "eval": eval_spec,
            "plan": plan,
            "results": results,
            "stats": stats,
            "samples": [sample],
            "attachments": dict(self._attachments),
        }
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Add a stable version marker to the emitted dict (e.g. 'eval_log_version': '2' or an ai_crucible-namespaced 'crucible_schema': 'evallog/2') so a consumer can detect and branch on schema drift, and persisted provenance records are self-describing. Document the version bump policy. Cheap and purely additive.

domains.coverage_notes: Read all in-scope source (kernel.py, sandbox.py, budget.py, roles.py, puzzle.py, framing.py, engagement.py, types.py) plus the dependency surfaces the proactive lens touches (trace.py, scoring/judge_panel.py, scoring/oracle.py head) and the matching test files (test_kernel.py, test_budget.py, test_sandbox.py, test_puzzle.py). The domain is already STRONG on proactive robustness in several areas, which I am NOT flagging: load_puzzle has structured code/message/hint errors with RED tests for every gate (missing dir/meta/prompt, bad JSON, schema violation, oracle-key smuggling); sandbox has a 1 MiB per-stream output cap, cross-platform process-TREE kill on timeout (with an honest documented residual on deliberately-detached double-fork), path-confinement against ..-traversal/absolute/symlink escapes proven on every platform, and an idempotent cleanup compensator; budget governor validates threshold>=2, never trusts model self-report, and has RED tests for BUDGET/TIME/HARD_KILL including hard-kill-precedes-budget; the kernel enforces LIVE time budget per-turn AND at the attempt boundary, stamps terminated_by legibly with error TraceEvents, and re-asserts the sealed-boundary andon after every message-mutating turn. The engagement guard fails closed on ambiguous numeric leaks (documented, intentional). framing.build_scored_context is exhaustive over the StrEnum with a defensive ValueError for forward-compat. Numerical robustness is largely clean within this domain (the heavy stats live elsewhere); _aggregate_novelty correctly fails closed on zero cast votes and reduce_scores guards unhashable/non-numeric reducer inputs. The five findings concentrate where the gap actually is: the post-Solver partial-failure region of run_attempt (HIGH — oracle/panel failure discards an expensive run untraced; this is the single most consequential gap and is untested), single-judge-failure aborting the whole panel (MEDIUM), an unbounded read at the puzzle input boundary that contrasts with the sandbox's own care (MEDIUM), a tight-budget HARD_KILL misattribution observability gap (LOW), and a missing eval-log schema-version marker for forward-compat (LOW). No findings were invented to pad; defensive path-confinement, output caps, and andon termination are genuinely solid here.

findings.title: One judge raising aborts the entire panel: asyncio.gather has no return_exceptions, so an Ollama timeout/crash discards every healthy judge's verdict
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: In Phase 2 the judges are real cross-family local models (Qwen + Mistral + Command-R via ollama-intern + RTX 5090). If ANY one judge call raises mid-fan-out — Ollama daemon down, a model OOMs, an HTTP timeout, a single malformed response — asyncio.gather (no return_exceptions=True) re-raises the FIRST exception and the awaiting coroutine loses the results of every other judge that already succeeded. A 3-judge panel where 2 judges returned clean verdicts produces NO panel score at all; the attempt's novelty adjudication and panel verdict are lost, and the exception that propagates (e.g. a bare RuntimeError from the model adapter) does not name which judge/model failed. On an expensive long eval batch this turns a recoverable single-model hiccup into a total loss of the panel verdict for that attempt with no partial result and no diagnosable 'which judge died' signal.
findings.description: JudgePanel.score gathers all eligible judges with `scores = await asyncio.gather(*(judge(attempt) for judge in eligible))`. With the default `return_exceptions=False`, the first judge coroutine to raise causes gather to propagate that exception immediately and the other (possibly already-completed) judge results are thrown away. There is no per-judge try/except, no naming of the offending judge in the error, and no path to a degraded/partial panel verdict over the judges that DID respond. The module docstring explicitly anticipates real cross-family local models over Ollama in Phase 2 — exactly the runtime where transient single-model failures are normal — yet the panel has no partial-failure handling. The empty-panel ValueError is legible, but a *partial*-panel failure is not. PoLL's whole premise is that a panel is robust to a single bad judge; an unhandled raise makes the panel LESS robust than a single judge (which at least either returns or fails atomically).
findings.evidence:
eligible = self.eligible_judges()
        if not eligible:
            raise ValueError(
                "no eligible judges remain after excluding generator family "
                ...
            )

        # One observable fan-out; judges are independent so we gather concurrently.
        scores = await asyncio.gather(*(judge(attempt) for judge in eligible))

        panel = reduce_scores(list(scores), self.reducer)
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Gather with `return_exceptions=True`, then partition results into (healthy scores, failed judges). Reduce over the healthy scores if quorum still holds (>=1, ideally >=PoLL min); record the failed judges' model ids + exception repr in panel.metadata under e.g. `judge_failures` and a `degraded: True` flag so a partial run is distinguishable from a clean one. If too few judges survive to meet quorum, raise a structured error that NAMES the failed judges and the surviving count (code/message/hint), rather than propagating the raw adapter exception. This mirrors the existing empty-panel legibility for the partial-failure case the docstring promises Phase 2 will hit.

findings.title: median reducer over a NaN judge value returns an order-dependent NaN that silently poisons the panel score
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: A real numeric judge (Phase 2: a model scoring 0.0-1.0) that returns NaN — a parse failure of the model's score, a 0/0 internal computation, or a non-finite confidence — flows untouched through `float(v)` (float('nan') succeeds) into `statistics.median`. The reduced panel `Score.value` becomes NaN whose VALUE depends on the judge ordering (proven: [1.0, NaN, 3.0]->NaN but [NaN, 1.0, 3.0]->1.0), because statistics.median sorts and NaN breaks the total order. That NaN then rides into the panel score and any downstream leaderboard/threshold comparison, where every comparison against it is silently False — a degraded judge is reported as a clean numeric verdict with no sentinel and no 'this panel had a non-finite vote' reason. The order-dependence also breaks the PIN_PER_STEP replayability the module claims (same inputs, different reduced value depending on seat order).
findings.description: reduce_scores('median') coerces each value via `float(v)` (which accepts 'nan'/'inf' strings and NaN floats without error) then calls statistics.median. There is no finite-check on the inputs and no guard that the median is finite. statistics.median does not define a total order over NaN, so the result is order-sensitive and non-deterministic w.r.t. judge ordering. The majority/weighted reducers have analogous but milder NaN exposure (NaN as a discrete vote tallies oddly because NaN != NaN), but median is the documented path for the numeric scores Phase 2 judges emit, so it is the realistic poison vector. Tests cover well-formed numeric medians (test_panel_median_reducer, test_reduce_median_numeric) but no NaN/inf input.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: After coercion, reject non-finite inputs with a structured ValueError that names the offending judge index/value (e.g. `import math; bad = [i for i,x in enumerate(numeric) if not math.isfinite(x)]` -> raise 'median reducer received non-finite value(s) from judge(s) {bad}'), turning a silent order-dependent NaN into a legible degenerate-input error. This matches the surrounding module's structured-error discipline and restores PIN_PER_STEP determinism.

findings.title: Oracle gate accepts NaN/inf solve_quality and time_used: NaN passes every threshold comparison and emits a non-finite Score.value with no failed condition
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: OracleOutcome is an unvalidated dataclass; nothing rejects a non-finite solve_quality/time_used coming from a puzzle's execution-based eval script (a 0/0 graded-quality computation, a parse-to-float of a junk model field, a runaway timer). Because every comparison against NaN is False, a NaN solve_quality does NOT trip `below_point_threshold` and a NaN time_used does NOT trip `over_time_budget` — the gate reports gate_passed=True with failed_conditions=[] (both proven), and the net Score.value is emitted as NaN (or +inf for inf solve_quality, also proven). That non-finite headline value then rides into the leaderboard net/tiebreaker and the median panel reducer (finding 002) as a clean-looking score. The solve-RATE rollups are protected because observability keys on the boolean gate_passed flag, not value — so this is a silently-wrong leaderboard *value* + a NaN that can poison numeric aggregation, not corrupted solve rates. The attestation event store WOULD reject the NaN at write time (test_append_rejects_non_finite_float_nan), so the poison is caught at persistence — which bounds the blast radius but means a 'passed' gate can carry a value the auditor's own store refuses to record.
findings.description: grade() compares outcome.solve_quality and outcome.time_used directly against thresholds with `<` and `>`. OracleOutcome (oracle.py:48) performs no finite-validation on these floats, and there is no isfinite guard in grade(). A NaN slips past every conjunctive condition (NaN < threshold is False; NaN > budget is False), so the gate opens and value becomes NaN; an inf solve_quality opens the gate and value becomes +inf. The explanation/metadata then claim a clean pass. The conjunctive gate is explicitly the 'headline' the design leans on for legibility, so a non-finite input producing a 'gate passed, value=NaN' record is exactly the silently-wrong-but-looks-clean case the proactive lens targets.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Validate the float fields at the trust boundary: either in OracleOutcome.__post_init__ (reject non-finite solve_quality/time_used with a structured ValueError naming the field), or as a guard at the top of grade() that appends an explicit `non_finite_outcome` failed-condition (failing the gate closed) when math.isfinite(solve_quality)/isfinite(time_used) is False. Failing closed on non-finite input is the conservative choice and keeps the gate's 'clean pass' claim honest; it also prevents handing a NaN into the leaderboard/median path.

domains.coverage_notes:
Files read in full (source + tests): scoring/stats.py, scoring/oracle.py, scoring/judge_panel.py, observability.py, plus tests/test_scoring.py and tests/test_observability.py. Also read characterize/aggregate.py (reliability_weighted_vote / compose_panel, called by the weighted reducer) and types.py (Score / OracleOutcome-adjacent contracts) to trace data flow. All probes run against the project .venv (scipy present); read-only, no mutations.

stats.py is genuinely robust and is a clean sub-domain — I invented no findings there. Verified the degenerate paths the prompt flagged: McNemar with zero discordant pairs returns 1.0 (guarded, tested); pass_hat_k handles 0/n, n/n, n=1 (p**k, no div-by-zero beyond the _validate_counts n<=0 guard); the elegance ratio canonical_call_count==0 case is unreachable as a div-by-zero because the `used > 0 and used <= canonical` guard forces canonical >= 1 before `(canonical-used)/canonical` runs; Wilson/Clopper-Pearson clamp to [0,1] and guard conf in (0,1); conformal_coverage_interval guards n<=0/alpha/conf and has an explicit ell<1 degenerate sentinel with a float-nudge fix already in place. The float-precision gate boundaries (>=, <=) are exercised by test_point_threshold_is_inclusive / test_tool_budget_is_inclusive and behave correctly at exact equality (no epsilon-comparison bug — these are intended inclusive boundaries). No ECE/IRT/kappa/beta-binomial/BH-FDR code exists in these four files (those are referenced in the prompt's general lens but live elsewhere or are deferred per stats.py docstrings), so nothing to audit there in-scope.

observability.py solve detection is robust: _attempt_solved keys on the boolean gate_passed flag (scoring-stats-001 fix), so a NaN Score.value does NOT corrupt solve_rate / pass^k / graduation rollups (verified by probe). solve_rate/novelty_rate/mean_latency all guard n_attempts==0. wilson_interval(0,0) returns the uninformative sentinel rather than dividing by zero (tested). The two pass^k implementations now share the k>=1 raise contract (scoring-stats-004, tested).

The three findings cluster on the partial-failure + non-finite-input seams the small-N stats core itself avoids: (001) panel partial-failure via asyncio.gather is the strongest — it directly contradicts the module's own Phase-2 'real Ollama models' promise and a single transient model failure loses an entire panel verdict with no naming; (002) median NaN poison is order-dependent and breaks the claimed PIN_PER_STEP determinism; (003) oracle gate accepts non-finite outcome fields and emits a 'clean pass' with a NaN/inf value. 002 and 003 are calibrated MEDIUM (not HIGH) because the solve-rate rollups are protected by the gate-flag path and the attestation store rejects non-finite floats at persistence, bounding the blast radius to leaderboard values + numeric aggregation rather than corrupted solve rates. 001 is HIGH: it is a realistic operational condition (one of three local judges hiccups) causing total loss of the panel verdict with a non-diagnosable raw exception.

findings.title: Failed models vanish from the report — a partial panel is indistinguishable from a smaller-by-design panel
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: On the ~2.6h Omen run, if 2 of the 6 panel models error or time out mid-run, the JSON report contains no record that they were ever attempted. A downstream consumer (or the operator weeks later) reads a 4-judge composed panel that 'met quorum' and treats it as the intended instrument — when in fact a third of the cross-family panel silently dropped out. The EXTERNAL_VERIFIER family diversity the panel exists to guarantee may be quietly gone (e.g. the only mistral-family or only cohere-family judge was one of the failures), and nothing in the persisted artifact flags it. This is silent degradation of the very instrument config the run produces.
findings.description: run_panel catches a per-model exception, prints '[model_id] ERROR: ...' to stdout, evicts, and `continue`s (lines 231-234). The failed model is never added to all_records, so it appears in neither `profiles`, `records`, nor anywhere in the report dict assembled at lines 460-513. The report has fields for `not_seated` (screen/reject) and `dropped_redundant` (ρ-pruned) inside panel_composition, but there is NO field listing models that were in the requested panel and failed to produce any judgments. The only total-failure guard (lines 523-538) fires when EVERY model fails; a PARTIAL failure (the common case: one model OOMs, one tag is unservable) produces a clean-looking report. The stdout print is the only trace, and stdout is not captured into the committed artifact. The panel composition `notes` say e.g. '4 judge(s) seated from 4 admitted' with no hint that 6 were requested.
findings.evidence:
try:
            recs = await collect_records(model, items, k=k)
        except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — one bad model must not sink the panel
            print(f"[{model_id}] ERROR: {exc!r}")
            _evict(model_id)
            continue
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - t0
        all_records[model_id] = recs
...
    report = {
        "n_items": len(items),
        "k": args.k,
        "item_set": ...,
        "alt_test_reference": ...,
        # (no "failed_models" / "attempted_panel" field anywhere)
        "profiles": {m: asdict(p) for m, p in profiles.items()},
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Collect failures into a list `failed: list[dict] = []` in run_panel (append {model_id, family, error: repr(exc), n_records_lost}) and thread it out alongside profiles/records. Add a top-level `"failed_models": failed` and `"attempted_panel": [m for m,_,_ in panel]` to the report dict. In compose_panel / panel_composition_report, record `requested_count` vs `seated_count` so a sub-quorum escalation note can say 'thinned by N failures' vs 'thin by design'. This is honesty-only — no fabricated data, just surfacing what was attempted vs measured.

findings.title: collect_records discards a model's entire transcript on any single-item failure (no partial salvage)
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: collect_records builds `records` internally and only returns it after the full items×k loop completes. If item 18 of 20 raises (a transient httpx timeout, a momentary OOM, a ModelMismatchError on a daemon hiccup) on a model that has otherwise judged 17 items × k=3 = 51 records perfectly, the exception propagates out, run_panel's except swallows it, and ALL 51 good records are thrown away — the model contributes nothing. On a ~25-min-per-model run that is ~22 minutes of correct local-GPU work lost to one bad item near the end, and (combined with finding 001) it vanishes with no diagnosable per-item cause in the artifact.
findings.description: The whole-model try/except in run_panel is documented as 'one bad model must not sink the panel', but the granularity is wrong: it makes one bad ITEM sink the whole MODEL. There is no per-item try/except inside collect_records, so a single judge_item raise loses every record accumulated so far for that model. A judge profile can be built from a partial item set (the metrics tolerate any non-empty record list, and n_items is derived from the distinct item_ids present), so salvaging 17/20 items would still yield a usable profile with a slightly wider Wilson CI — strictly better than dropping the model.
findings.evidence:
records: list[JudgmentRecord] = []
    for item in items:
        gold_str = str(item.gold).upper()
        gold_num = _to_num(gold_str)
        for ri in range(k):
            rec = await model.judge_item(item.prompt, run_index=ri)
            rec.item_id = item.id  # authored id, not the prompt-hash fallback (§11.3)
            ...
            records.append(rec)
    return records
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Wrap the inner `rec = await model.judge_item(...)` (or the per-item block) in a try/except that on failure logs the item_id + error, increments a dropped-item counter, and `continue`s to the next item, rather than letting it unwind the whole model. Return the partial records plus a count of dropped items so build_profile / the report can note 'profiled on 17/20 items (3 dropped: <ids>, <reason>)'. Optionally bound consecutive failures (e.g. abort the model only after N consecutive item failures, which genuinely signals the daemon is down).

findings.title: No checkpoint/resume of the multi-hour run — a crash at model 5/6 is a total loss
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: all_records is an in-memory dict accumulated across the sequential load→judge→evict loop; nothing is persisted until the single report.write_text at the very end (line 514). A process crash, OOM kill, power blip, SSH drop, or Ctrl-C at model 5 of 6 — ~2 hours into a ~2.6h run — loses every judged record for all five completed models. The operator must re-run the entire panel from model 1, re-spending the GPU hours already paid. Because the whole report is written atomically at the end, there is no intermediate artifact to restart from.
findings.description: run_panel does pass 1 (judge the whole panel, accumulating into all_records) entirely before pass 2 (profiling) and before any disk write. There is no per-model checkpoint of the collected JudgmentRecords, and main() offers no --resume that would skip already-judged models. The cost asymmetry is exactly what the proactive-degradation lens warns about: the run is long and expensive, the failure mode (process death) is realistic, and the recovery is 'start over'. panel_store.save_panel exists for the FINAL composed panel but not for the intermediate raw records that are the expensive part.
findings.evidence:
all_records: dict[str, list[JudgmentRecord]] = {}
    quant_by_id: dict[str, str | None] = {}
    for model_id, family, quant in panel:
        ...
        try:
            recs = await collect_records(model, items, k=k)
        except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001
            print(f"[{model_id}] ERROR: {exc!r}")
            _evict(model_id)
            continue
        ...
        all_records[model_id] = recs
        ...
    # (records only ever hit disk via report.write_text at the very end of main)
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: After each model finishes collect_records, write its records to a per-model JSONL/JSON checkpoint (e.g. <out>.records/<model_id>.json) before moving on. Add a --resume flag that loads existing per-model checkpoints and skips those models in pass 1. Pass 2 (profiling) then runs over the union of fresh + resumed records. This makes a crash cost at most one model's worth of work and is a natural fit with the existing PIN_PER_STEP/replayability framing.

findings.title: Per-item parse failures are scored as wrong and never aggregated into a legible signal
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: When a model emits output that parse_choice cannot resolve to a verdict (it returned prose, refused, emitted both letters, or the prompt format drifted), collect_records assigns `pred_num = 1 - gold_num` — i.e. it counts the item as definitively WRONG — and stamps raw_choice=None into per-record metadata. The operator looking at the report sees a low objective_accuracy / a REJECT decision with no way to distinguish 'this model genuinely judges badly' from 'this model's output format wasn't parseable and we scored every unparseable item as a miss'. A format/prompt-template regression that breaks parsing for one model would masquerade as that model failing the admission gate — a measurement-biasing condition that is invisible at the report level (only recoverable by hand-grepping raw_choice across thousands of records, which the report does not persist).
findings.description: This is the Stage-A-noted measurement-biasing branch (line 153). The data is technically captured in rec.metadata['raw_choice'] = None per record, but: (a) the per-model print line (line 238) shows only acc=..., not a parse-failure rate; (b) the report JSON profiles carry no parse-failure count; (c) the records themselves are not persisted to the artifact. So there is no operator-facing signal that says 'model X had 11/60 unparseable outputs scored as wrong'. The choice to map unparseable -> wrong is defensible as a conservative default, but conflating 'unparseable' with 'incorrect' without surfacing the unparseable rate makes a degraded/format-broken run indistinguishable from a genuinely-weak judge in the JudgeProfile output.
findings.evidence:
parsed = parse_choice(str(rec.predicted), gold_str)
            pred_num = _to_num(parsed) if parsed is not None else (1 - gold_num)
            rec.predicted = pred_num
            rec.gold = gold_num
            rec.correct = parsed == gold_str
            rec.metadata["category"] = item.category.value
            rec.metadata["raw_choice"] = parsed
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Count parse failures per model in collect_records (n where parsed is None) and surface it: include it in the '[model_id] judged N records' print and add `parse_failure_rate` / `n_unparsed` to each JudgeProfile.metadata (or a per-model entry in the report). Then build_profile/the report can emit a note like 'WARNING: 18% of outputs were unparseable and scored as incorrect — check the prompt format before trusting this REJECT'. Honesty-only: no scoring change required, just making the unparsed count a first-class, report-visible signal so a format break is diagnosable rather than silently depressing accuracy.

findings.title: No per-item timeout/retry around judge_item — a single hung Ollama call can stall the whole run with no recovery
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: judge_item awaits a single Ollama chat call whose httpx fallback timeout is 600s (ollama_adapter.py line 366). If the daemon wedges on one item (a known failure mode under load→evict churn on a 32GB box), that one call blocks up to 10 minutes with no asyncio.wait_for guard, no retry, and no backoff at the orchestration layer. Across items×k×models a few wedged calls add tens of minutes of dead wait; a fully hung daemon blocks the run for the full 600s per stuck call before the exception propagates and the model is dropped (losing all its work per finding 002). There is no orchestration-level retry/backoff to ride out a transient daemon hiccup.
findings.description: collect_records calls `await model.judge_item(...)` with no asyncio.wait_for, no retry, and no backoff. The only timeout is the adapter's transport-level 600s (and the official ollama SDK path has no explicit timeout at all in _resolve_client). For the load→judge→evict topology on a single GPU where the daemon can transiently stall during a model swap, a single retry-with-backoff would let the run ride out a hiccup instead of either hanging for 10 minutes or dropping the model entirely. This is LOW because it does not corrupt data — the worst case is wasted wall-clock and a dropped model — but it directly undermines graceful degradation of the expensive long run.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Wrap judge_item in asyncio.wait_for(..., timeout=<per-item budget>) and a small bounded retry-with-backoff (e.g. 2 retries, exponential) so a transient daemon stall is ridden out rather than hanging for the full transport timeout or sinking the model. Pin the per-item timeout in metadata for PIN_PER_STEP. Combine with finding 002's per-item except so a final give-up drops only the item, not the model.

domains.coverage_notes:
Scope read in full: run.py, metrics.py, aggregate.py, profile.py, human_labels.py, panel_store.py + tests (test_characterize_run.py, test_characterize.py header) and the supporting types.py + ollama_adapter.py (to understand the actual failure surface of judge_item). Per the dispatch, I deliberately did NOT re-report Stage-A bug/correctness/eval-integrity findings, and I treated scoring-numerics (NaN/inf/div-by-zero in Wilson/kappa/ECE/IRT/alt-test) as another domain's lens — those metric edge cases (kappa NaN->1.0, pearson zero-variance->0.0, ECE None, alt_test degenerate-fold guards, krippendorff <2 values->1.0) are already handled defensively and were not findings here.

Clean sub-domains (genuinely good degradation/observability — no findings):
- human_labels.py: exemplary defensive loading. Structured [CODE] message (hint:) errors at every trust boundary (missing path, bad JSON, wrong shape, unknown item/annotator/tier, bad verdict typo NOT silently coerced, too-few annotators). Under-power (n<30), low-IAA epsilon clamp, and DISPUTED drops are all surfaced as honest notes. Strong work.
- panel_store.py: forward-compat is handled correctly — load_panel REFUSES a schema_version newer than SCHEMA_VERSION (the future-proofing the prompt asked about) and rejects unknown seat keys, missing version, and mistyped fields with structured errors. This is the right pattern; no gap.
- aggregate.py: degenerate guards are thorough (single-judge panel vacuously passes, zero-variance error vectors -> 0.0/independent, <2 shared items handled, empty corr handled, threshold range-checked, sub-quorum -> escalate rather than seat thin). compose_panel is pure/deterministic. The one observability gap (requested-vs-seated count) is folded into finding 001.

The five findings concentrate where the dispatch pointed: ORCHESTRATION robustness of the long/expensive run in run.py. The single HIGH (001) is the load-bearing one — partial model failure is operationally realistic and currently produces a clean-looking artifact that silently misrepresents the seated instrument (and can silently break EXTERNAL_VERIFIER family diversity). 002/003/004 are MEDIUM (work/time loss + a measurement-biasing observability gap, all recoverable/diagnosable in principle but not surfaced). 005 is LOW (wasted wall-clock, no data corruption). A cross-family verifier should pressure-test 001's severity vs 002/003: I rated 001 HIGH because the wrong/degraded panel can be CONSUMED as valid (it passes the non-empty guard and writes a normal report), whereas 002/003 are loss-of-work that the operator at least notices via a crash or a missing model. No findings were invented to fill the domain; the defensive/forward-compat sub-domains are legitimately clean.

findings.title: No retry/backoff on any model call — a single transient blip aborts a model's entire multi-hour characterization run
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: During the §11.2 sequential load->judge->evict run (6 local models x ~60 items x k=3 ≈ hundreds of sequential calls per model, the documented 2.6h-class run), a single transient failure — a momentary connection reset while a 24-35B model is mid-load, a one-off socket hiccup, a brief daemon stall — raises out of judge_item with no retry. In the documented consumer (characterize/run.py:collect_records, called inside the per-model try/except at run.py:229) that exception is caught at the WHOLE-MODEL granularity: every record already collected for that model (e.g. 47 of 60 items, runs 0-1 of 2 done) is discarded and the model is dropped from the panel entirely. The failure is printed (run.py:232) and an all-failed run exits non-zero (run.py:523), so it is NOT silent — but a recoverable one-call blip costs that model its entire (expensive, GPU-bound, irreproducible-at-zero-cost) contribution to the run. There is no idempotent single-item retry, no bounded backoff, no transient-vs-permanent distinction anywhere in the model edge.
findings.description: Neither adapter implements any retry/backoff/transient-error recovery. The Ollama httpx fallback (_via_httpx) does a single POST, raise_for_status(), and returns — a connection reset, a 503 while a model loads, or a read hiccup propagates immediately. The Ollama SDK path (_via_sdk) and the Claude path (complete) likewise make exactly one call with no retry wrapper. The Anthropic SDK does have its own internal retry default, so Claude gets *some* transient coverage for free, but the Ollama edge — the one the harness leans on hardest for the long sequential run — has none. The harness's per-model try/except is graceful at the PANEL level (one bad model doesn't sink the run) but is coarse: it cannot distinguish 'this model is genuinely unservable' from 'one call out of hundreds glitched', so a transient blip is upgraded into total loss of that model's run. A bounded single-item retry (e.g. 2-3 attempts with exponential backoff on connection/5xx errors, never on a 4xx or a ModelMismatchError) at the judge_item/_complete_raw layer would convert the common transient case from 'lose the whole model' to 'lose ~1s'. Not HIGH because the loss is diagnosable (printed + non-zero exit) and bounded to one model, not silent corruption.
findings.evidence:
import httpx

        async def _via_httpx(**kwargs: Any) -> dict[str, Any]:
            payload = {**kwargs, "stream": False}
            async with httpx.AsyncClient(base_url=host, timeout=600.0) as http:
                r = await http.post("/api/chat", json=payload)
                r.raise_for_status()
                return r.json()

        self._client = _via_httpx
        return self._client
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Add a small bounded retry helper around the single request in _complete_raw (Ollama) and complete (Claude): retry on httpx.ConnectError / httpx.ReadError / httpx.RemoteProtocolError and on 5xx (httpx.HTTPStatusError where 500 <= status < 600), with exponential backoff (e.g. 0.5s, 2s, 8s) and a small max attempt count (3). NEVER retry a 4xx, a ModelMismatchError, or a successful-but-empty completion (those are not transient). Make the policy a constructor knob (max_retries=2, retry_backoff_base=0.5) so it is pinned per step (PIN_PER_STEP) and recordable in pin_metadata. For the harness, consider retrying at the single-item granularity in collect_records so a blip costs one item, not a whole model.

findings.title: Ollama daemon-down / unreachable surfaces as a bare httpx ConnectError with no operator hint
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: The single most common operational failure for this edge is 'the operator forgot to start ollama serve' or 'OLLAMA_HOST points at the wrong box'. When that happens, the first call raises a raw httpx.ConnectError ('All connection attempts failed' / 'Connection refused') from inside _via_httpx — or an opaque error from the ollama SDK on its path. The adapter adds zero context: it does not say which host it tried, does not name the model, and gives no hint ('is the Ollama daemon running on http://localhost:11434?'). The run.py consumer prints `[{model_id}] ERROR: {exc!r}` (good — the model is named there) and the all-failed path emits a structured code/message/hint (run.py:524 CHARACTERIZE_NO_JUDGMENTS, which DOES name the daemon + host) — so at the top level the operator is reasonably served. The residual gap is anyone calling the adapter directly (kernel generate, a panel judge outside characterize/run.py, an ad-hoc script): they get the bare transport stack with no hint and must already know the host/daemon contract to diagnose it.
findings.description: The shipcheck Gate-B standard for this repo requires structured errors with code/message/hint and 'no raw stacks'. The characterize/run.py all-failed path meets that bar. But the adapter itself — the reusable library surface used by the kernel and any future caller — lets the raw httpx/SDK transport exception escape with no wrapping. There is no OllamaUnreachableError analogous to the well-built ModelMismatchError (which DOES carry requested/served + a §-cited message). The host is resolved at line 344 but is not attached to any error if the connect fails. A caller that is not characterize/run.py gets a bare 'Connection refused' with no indication that the fix is `ollama serve` or a corrected OLLAMA_HOST. ModelMismatchError shows the right pattern for this codebase (named exception, structured message, hint embedded); the connect/transport failure deserves the same treatment.
findings.evidence:
host = self._host or os.environ.get("OLLAMA_HOST", _DEFAULT_HOST)

        try:
            import ollama  # type: ignore[import-untyped]

            async_client = ollama.AsyncClient(host=host)
            ...
        except ImportError:
            pass

        import httpx

        async def _via_httpx(**kwargs: Any) -> dict[str, Any]:
            payload = {**kwargs, "stream": False}
            async with httpx.AsyncClient(base_url=host, timeout=600.0) as http:
                r = await http.post("/api/chat", json=payload)
                r.raise_for_status()
                return r.json()
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Wrap the POST in _via_httpx (and ideally the SDK call) to catch httpx.ConnectError / httpx.ConnectTimeout and re-raise as a structured OllamaUnreachableError(host, model_id) whose message names the host and model and carries a hint ('is the Ollama daemon running and reachable at {host}? start it with `ollama serve`, or set OLLAMA_HOST'). Mirror ModelMismatchError's shape (RuntimeError subclass with named fields + §-cited, hint-bearing message). This makes a direct adapter call as diagnosable as the characterize/run.py top-level path already is.

findings.title: Malformed Ollama HTTP-200 body raises an opaque JSONDecodeError; no guard that the response is even a mapping
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: Ollama (or a reverse-proxy / load-balancer in front of OLLAMA_HOST) can return HTTP 200 with a body that is not the expected JSON object — an HTML error page from an intermediary, a truncated/streamed fragment if `stream` defaulting ever drifts, or a JSON array/string instead of an object. On the httpx path r.json() then raises a raw json.JSONDecodeError (or httpx.DecodingError) with no model/host context, indistinguishable from a code bug. If a non-object JSON (a list or a bare string) DID decode, the very next line is _assert_served_matches(response) calling response.get('model') — and every downstream helper (_extract_text, _first_token_logprob) calls response.get(...) — which would AttributeError on a non-dict. The adapter never validates that the decoded response is a mapping before treating it as one. The blast radius is one model's run (caught at run.py's per-model try/except), so it is not a silent-corruption HIGH; but the operator gets a misleading low-level parse/attribute error rather than 'model X returned a non-JSON/non-object body'.
findings.description: _via_httpx trusts the body to be JSON and an object. _complete_raw and the _extract_text / _first_token_logprob helpers all assume `response` is a dict (they call .get). There is no `isinstance(response, dict)` guard at the boundary and no try/except around r.json(). A well-built adapter at a trust boundary (a network reply from a daemon that may be fronted by infrastructure the operator doesn't control) should validate the shape it parses and fail with a legible, model-named error. Note this is the inverse of the careful defensive work already done inside _extract_text/_logprob_of (which tolerate MISSING fields gracefully) — the gap is specifically a response that is the WRONG TYPE, not a dict with missing keys.
findings.evidence:
r = await http.post("/api/chat", json=payload)
                r.raise_for_status()
                return r.json()
...
    async def _complete_raw(self, messages: list[dict[str, Any]]) -> dict[str, Any]:
        client = self._resolve_client()
        response = await client(
            model=self.model_id,
            messages=messages,
            options=self._options(),
            **self._logprob_request(),
        )
        self._assert_served_matches(response)
        return response
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: In _via_httpx, wrap r.json() in try/except (json.JSONDecodeError / httpx.DecodingError) and re-raise a structured OllamaBadResponseError(model_id, host, snippet_of_body) with a hint that an intermediary may be returning a non-JSON body. Add a cheap `if not isinstance(response, dict): raise OllamaBadResponseError(...)` at the top of _complete_raw so a non-object reply fails with a model-named message instead of an AttributeError 20 lines later in _extract_text. Add a test feeding a fake client that returns a list / a bare string / raises a decode error.

findings.title: _via_sdk assumes the ollama SDK response is dict()-convertible; a SDK shape change raises an opaque TypeError
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: The ollama Python SDK is an unpinned external dependency (resolved lazily, not version-bounded by this module). _via_sdk normalizes the SDK's pydantic-ish response with `dict(resp) if not isinstance(resp, dict) else resp`. `dict(obj)` only works if obj is a mapping or an iterable of key/value pairs. Current ollama ChatResponse objects happen to support this, but a future SDK major could return an object that is neither a dict nor dict()-able (e.g. a plain pydantic model without __iter__ yielding pairs), at which point `dict(resp)` raises a bare `TypeError: cannot convert dictionary update sequence...` with no indication that the cause is an SDK version drift. Low severity because it surfaces immediately and loudly on the first call (not silent, not data-corrupting) and only affects the real-SDK path (tests use fakes), but the error would mislead an operator into suspecting their own code rather than an SDK upgrade.
findings.description: The normalization is a reasonable best-effort, but it bakes in an assumption about the SDK's response type that is neither version-pinned nor defensively checked. Since the module deliberately resolves `ollama` lazily and never constrains its version, a future SDK shape is a real (if low-probability) drift surface. A model_dump()/dict()/getattr-fallback ladder with a clear error would be more forward-compatible. This is the only place the adapter touches a third-party response object's structure rather than a plain mapping.
findings.evidence:
async def _via_sdk(**kwargs: Any) -> dict[str, Any]:
                resp = await async_client.chat(**kwargs)
                # The SDK may return a pydantic-ish object; normalize to a mapping the
                # rest of the adapter (and tests) treat uniformly.
                return dict(resp) if not isinstance(resp, dict) else resp
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Make the normalization tolerant of more SDK shapes and fail legibly: try in order — isinstance dict -> as-is; hasattr 'model_dump' (pydantic v2) -> resp.model_dump(); hasattr 'dict' -> resp.dict(); else try dict(resp) inside a try/except TypeError that re-raises a structured error naming the installed ollama version (importlib.metadata.version('ollama')) and the unexpected type. Cheap and removes the silent assumption.

domains.coverage_notes:
SCOPE: src/ai_crucible/models/ollama_adapter.py, claude_adapter.py, tests/test_models.py. Also read (for consumer/severity context, not in scope for findings): scoring/judge_panel.py, characterize/profile.py, characterize/run.py, observability.py, models/__init__.py. Confirmed via grep that there is ZERO retry/backoff/rate-limit/timeout-config code anywhere in src/ai_crucible/models, and no documented resilience-deferral stance in docs/ or SECURITY.md (so the retry gap is a genuine omission, not a deliberate deferral).

SEVERITY CALIBRATION (honest, for the cross-family verifier): I found NO HIGH. The strongest candidate was 'a transient blip kills a 2.6h run with no diagnosable cause' (finding 001), but it is MEDIUM not HIGH because the documented consumer (characterize/run.py) already (a) wraps each model in try/except so one failure can't sink the panel (line 229), (b) names the failing model on stderr (line 232), and (c) emits a structured code/message/hint (CHARACTERIZE_NO_JUDGMENTS) and exits NON-ZERO when every model fails (line 523-538). So a transient failure is diagnosable and bounded, never silent wrong data nor an unrecoverable hang. The residual cost — losing one model's whole already-collected record set to a single recoverable blip — is real and operationally annoying (expensive GPU time, irreproducible) but not data corruption.

WELL-HANDLED (deliberately NOT reported as findings — these are correct):
- The served-vs-requested provenance guard (ModelMismatchError + _norm_model + _assert_served_matches) is exemplary: named exception with requested/served fields, §-cited hint-bearing message, fails CLOSED only on a positive mismatch, tolerates a missing model field for older servers/fakes, normalizes cloud/:latest suffixes, and degrades sensibly in a panel (the mis-served judge raises -> caught per-model in run.py -> that judge dropped with a named reason, never silently mis-attributed). Stage A's models-cli-002 work here is solid and well-tested (8 dedicated tests covering mismatch/match/cloud-suffix/missing-field on both adapters).
- Numerical robustness on the logprob/confidence path is genuinely careful: _logprob_of rejects bool (the int-subclass trap), _logprob_to_probability clamps exp() overshoot to [0,1] AND has a test for fp noise at logprob≈0 (test_judge_item_confidence_clamped_to_unit_interval), None-in->None-out so a logprob-less server never fabricates a confidence (test_judge_item_confidence_none_when_logprobs_absent). math.exp of a very negative logprob underflows to 0.0 cleanly (no exception). No NaN/inf/div0 hazards in this adapter's math.
- _extract_text on both adapters tolerates MISSING fields gracefully (never raises on absent message/content/response), returning '' as a legitimate empty completion. The Claude _extract_text handles three response shapes (str / dict-blocks / SDK-object-blocks) defensively.
- Secret handling (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY) is read at call time, never captured at import, never logged/stored — correct.

OBSERVABILITY GAP NOTED but kept in coverage rather than as a finding (the partial-failure path lives in judge_panel.py, outside my assigned files): JudgePanel.score uses `asyncio.gather(*(judge(attempt) ...))` WITHOUT return_exceptions=True (judge_panel.py:419). If ONE eligible judge raises (e.g. an OllamaModel judge hits a transient error or ModelMismatchError mid-panel), gather propagates the first exception and abandons the other judges' in-flight results — the panel returns NO verdict rather than degrading to the surviving cross-family judges. The adapters correctly raise (that is their contract), but the panel's lack of partial-failure tolerance means an adapter-edge transient on one judge aborts the whole panel score. Flagging for the scoring-domain auditor; not reported here because the file is out of my scope and the adapters' raising behavior is itself correct.

TEST COVERAGE OBSERVATION: tests/test_models.py is thorough on the happy path, determinism pinning, logprob shapes, family exclusion, and the served-mismatch provenance guard — but has ZERO tests exercising transport-failure paths: no test for a client that raises ConnectError/timeout, no test for a malformed/non-dict response body, no test for retry behavior (because there is none). This is consistent with build-law 3 (no network in tests) but means the resilience surface findings 001-003 describe is entirely unexercised — a fake client that raises, or returns a non-dict, would be a trivial and valuable addition alongside any fix.

findings.title: sobol_screen masks a NaN objective to T_i=0.0, silently freezing a load-bearing weight
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: The §9.4-step-2 sensitivity screen is the mechanism that decides which weights are tunable vs frozen. If the calibration surrogate model_fn returns NaN for some parameter region (a 0/0, log(0), or empty-sample edge — entirely plausible for a 'gate-clear rate as a function of weights' surrogate), scipy returns NaN total_order for that parameter, and the clip `max(0.0, total[i])` returns 0.0 (because Python's max returns its first argument when the comparison against NaN is False). The operator reads T_i=0.0, concludes the weight is 'not load-bearing,' FREEZES it, and never tunes it — a silently wrong screening verdict that mis-shapes the entire downstream BO search, with no NaN warning anywhere in the output or the TUNING.md provenance. An inf total_order is NOT clipped at all and passes straight through as a non-physical Sobol index.
findings.description: The return line `{name: float(max(0.0, total[i])) ...}` is meant to clip slightly-negative finite-sample Sobol estimates to 0.0. But `max(0.0, NaN)` evaluates to 0.0 (NaN comparisons are False, so max keeps the first operand), which is indistinguishable in the output from a legitimate zero-effect parameter. The function has no `np.isfinite` guard on `result.total_order`, so a NaN/inf objective never raises and never warns — it is laundered into a plausible-looking screen result. This contrasts with the sibling rubric_bundle.canonical_bundle_json, which uses `allow_nan=False` precisely to refuse NaN. A verifier confirmed: with a NaN-returning model_fn, sobol_screen returns {'a': 0.0, 'b': 0.0}; with inf, it returns inf unclipped. Severity is MEDIUM not HIGH because it requires the operator's surrogate to actually produce a non-finite value, and the downstream effect is a mis-tuned (not corrupted) instrument that the validation-split check (step 5) would catch — but the screen result itself is silently wrong with no diagnostic.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Before clipping, assert finiteness and raise a structured TuningError naming the offending parameter(s): `bad = [names[i] for i in range(len(names)) if not np.isfinite(total[i])]; if bad: raise _fail('STATE_SOBOL_NONFINITE', f'Sobol total-effect is non-finite for {bad}', 'the objective model_fn returned NaN/inf over the sample — check the surrogate for 0/0, log(0), or empty-region evaluations before screening')`. Then clip the remaining finite values with `float(np.clip(total[i], 0.0, None))` (np.clip propagates NaN correctly and never masks it, unlike the order-dependent builtin max).

findings.title: inspect-ai pinned only >=0.3 with no upper bound and no version assertion; Sample.model_dump shape is consumed wholesale
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: pyproject pins `inspect-ai>=0.3` with no upper bound; inspect-ai is a fast-moving pre-1.0 framework (0.3.233 installed). to_inspect_task constructs a real `inspect_ai.dataset.Sample(...)` and emits `sample.model_dump()` verbatim as the auditor-facing `dataset` payload — the published §9.6 task-definition artifact. If a future inspect-ai release renames/removes a Sample field, changes model_dump's key set, or changes the SandboxEnvironmentSpec 2-tuple convention, every emitted task definition silently changes shape (e.g. the verifier confirmed model_dump today emits 9 keys including 'choices','files','setup','checkpoint' that the docstring/tests never mention). An auditor who pinned ai-crucible's documented format but resolves a newer inspect-ai gets a divergent artifact, or — worse for forward-compat — to_inspect_task could begin emitting fields the published schema doc never described, with no error. There is no `inspect_ai.__version__` assertion and no schema-version stamp on the emitted dict to let a consumer reject an unexpected shape.
findings.description: The whole credibility argument of this module (docstring: 'guaranteed compatible with the installed Inspect version rather than hand-faked') depends on the installed inspect-ai version, but nothing records or bounds which version produced the artifact. The emitted dict carries no producing-inspect-ai-version field and no format-version field, so a future consumer cannot tell whether a task definition came from inspect-ai 0.3 or a breaking 0.4/1.0. Combined with the unbounded `>=0.3` pin, this is the exact 'silent model-version drift' failure that the SUT module (sut.py docstring, arXiv:2510.25506) warns causes >40% of eval artifacts to fail within months — applied here to the framework rather than the model. Tests use a `sample_meta` fixture and assert specific keys but never pin or assert the inspect-ai version, so a CI upgrade to a breaking inspect-ai would either break opaquely or, if model_dump still succeeds, pass while emitting a silently-different artifact.
findings.evidence:
from inspect_ai.dataset import Sample
...
    sample = Sample(
        input=prompt,
        target="",  # sealed-oracle invariant: the Solver never sees the answer
        id=puzzle_meta.puzzle_id,
        metadata={...},
    )

    return {
        "name": f"ai_crucible-{puzzle_meta.puzzle_id}",
        "dataset": [sample.model_dump()],
        ...
        "sandbox": ["docker", None],
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: (1) Add an upper bound or a tested-compat range to the pin: `inspect-ai>=0.3,<0.4`. (2) Stamp the artifact with provenance the auditor can check: add `"_inspect_ai_version": importlib.metadata.version('inspect-ai')` and a `"_task_format_version": "1"` to the returned dict, so a consumer of a future schema can reject an unknown format cleanly instead of misparsing. (3) Optionally select only the documented Sample keys into `dataset` rather than emitting the full model_dump(), so an upstream field addition can't silently widen the published artifact.

findings.title: parse_sut_yaml silently accepts duplicate keys (last-wins) and ignores malformed indented/list YAML it cannot represent
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: parse_sut_yaml is the reference reader an external auditor uses to confirm what SUT was pinned (§9.6, frozen submission→publication). It is a hand-rolled line parser, not a real YAML parser. If a SUT.yaml is hand-edited (the file is meant to be human-readable) to contain a duplicate `model_id:` line, the parser silently keeps the LAST occurrence (`found[key] = value` overwrites with no detection) — so two conflicting model ids in the frozen record resolve to one with no error, and an auditor reading the file top-to-bottom may believe a different value was pinned than the parser returns. Any genuinely structured YAML (a value that is a list/map, a multiline scalar, an anchor) is either misparsed as a string or, if it lacks a top-level colon, rejected — but a duplicate scalar key is the realistic hand-edit failure and it passes silently.
findings.description: The parser loops lines and does `found[key] = value` with no duplicate-key guard, so a repeated field is last-wins rather than an error. For a frozen-reproducibility artifact where the discipline is 'the value an auditor reads back is the value that was recorded' (module docstring), silently collapsing a duplicate key undermines exactly that guarantee. It is LOW because the happy-path round-trip (render→parse) is tested and correct, render_sut_yaml never emits duplicates, and the realistic trigger is a manual edit of a file the module otherwise controls — no automated path produces silent wrong data. But the module advertises itself as the auditor's reference reader, so a defensive duplicate-key rejection would match its stated role.
findings.evidence:
key, _, value = line.partition(":")
        key = key.strip()
        value = value.strip()
        # Unwrap a double-quoted scalar.
        if len(value) >= 2 and value[0] == '"' and value[-1] == '"':
            value = value[1:-1].replace('\\"', '"').replace("\\\\", "\\")
        found[key] = value
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Reject a repeated known field with a structured error: `if key in SUT_FIELDS and key in found: raise _fail('INPUT_SUT_DUPLICATE_FIELD', f'SUT.yaml repeats field {key!r}', 'a frozen SUT must pin each field exactly once; remove the duplicate line')`. Optionally also reject keys outside SUT_FIELDS so a typo'd field (e.g. `model-id:` vs `model_id:`) surfaces rather than being silently dropped while the real field reads as missing.

findings.title: _looks_like_family_alias treats non-ASCII digits (and superscripts) as a valid exact version, weakening the §9.6 guardrail
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: The family-alias check is the §9.6 reproducibility guardrail that stops a non-pinned model id (e.g. 'claude-opus') from shipping in a frozen SUT. It uses `any(ch.isdigit() for ch in model_id)`, and Python's str.isdigit() is True for non-ASCII digits and some numeric glyphs (Arabic-Indic ١, superscript ²). A verifier confirmed `claude-opus-١` and `model-²` both pass as 'exact.' A model id that LOOKS like a family alias but happens to contain a stray non-ASCII numeric character would slip through the guardrail and ship in a frozen, published SUT — defeating the check's purpose for that record. The module itself already labels this 'a guardrail, not a proof,' so the worst case is a guardrail miss, not corrupted data.
findings.description: isdigit() is broader than ASCII [0-9]; an exact-version heuristic that only requires 'contains a digit' is already loose (the module acknowledges this), and the non-ASCII acceptance makes it looser in a way that is invisible to a maintainer reasoning about ASCII version strings. Genuine model ids are ASCII in practice so the realistic trigger is narrow, hence LOW. Worth a one-line tightening since the check is security-of-reproducibility flavored.
findings.evidence:
We treat an id as "exact" if it contains a digit (a date stamp like
    ``-20260415`` or a version like ``-4-7``). A bare family name
    (``claude-opus``, ``gpt``, ``mistral-large``) has none and is the §9.6
    failure mode. This is a guardrail, not a proof — an exact-looking id can
    still be wrong; the check catches the obvious, common mistake.
    """
    return not any(ch.isdigit() for ch in model_id)
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Restrict to ASCII digits: `return not any(ch in '0123456789' for ch in model_id)` (or `not re.search(r'[0-9]', model_id)`). Optionally strengthen the heuristic toward 'looks like a dated/versioned snapshot' (e.g. require a run of >=2 ASCII digits) so a single stray digit in a family name doesn't pass.

findings.title: calibration loader reads every file fully with no size or file-count bound on an operator-supplied directory
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: load_items accepts an arbitrary operator-supplied path, globs all `*.json` in a directory with no count cap, and reads each via `file.read_text(encoding='utf-8')` with no size cap before json.loads. Pointed at a pathologically large file or a directory with very many/large JSON files, the loader loads everything into memory at once and can exhaust RAM. There is no streaming and no early-size check. Because the calibration bank is operator-authored fixtures (the bundled bank is ~14 items) and not an untrusted network input, the realistic consequence is a high-memory failure on a misconfigured path, not silent wrong data — so LOW.
findings.description: Trust-boundary note: this is an operator-facing loader, not an adversarial-input surface, so unbounded reads are a robustness/respect gap rather than a vulnerability. A directory load concatenates every file's records into one list with no aggregate cap. A defensive size/count guard would turn a confusing OOM into a clear, structured error naming the offending file — matching the module's otherwise-strong structured-error discipline (every other failure here raises a coded CalibrationLoadError with a hint; only resource exhaustion fails opaquely).
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Add a soft cap with a structured error: before read_text, `if file.stat().st_size > MAX_CALIB_FILE_BYTES: raise _fail('INPUT_CALIB_FILE_TOO_LARGE', f'{file.name} is {size} bytes (> {MAX} cap)', 'split the calibration bank into per-item files or raise the cap deliberately')`, with MAX a generous default (e.g. 16 MiB). Optionally cap the file count in _read_dir similarly. This keeps the common path untouched while making a runaway path diagnosable.

domains.coverage_notes:
Scope read in full: instrument/{rubric_bundle,prereg,sut,tuning,inspect_task}.py, calibration/{loader,known_groups,irt,types}.py, tests/{test_instrument,test_calibration}.py, pyproject.toml. Verifier probes run against the repo's .venv (inspect-ai 0.3.233, scipy/statsmodels present): confirmed sobol_screen NaN->0.0 masking and inf pass-through (finding 001); confirmed Sample.model_dump() emits 9 keys incl. undocumented choices/files/setup/checkpoint and no version stamp (002); confirmed _looks_like_family_alias accepts non-ASCII digits (004); confirmed prune_items degrades cleanly (drops all, no crash) on a single-model matrix.

DOMAINS THAT CAME UP CLEAN (no findings, by design):
- OPTIONAL-DEPENDENCY HANDLING is exemplary. The two optional paths in scope handle absence correctly: calibration/irt.fit_irt_bayesian does a LAZY py-irt import inside the function and raises a clear NotImplementedError with the exact `uv add --optional irt py-irt` install hint (not a bare ImportError mid-run). The [stats] extra (statsmodels) is NOT imported by any file in my domain — grep confirms tuning.py uses scipy.stats only; statsmodels consumers (McNemar/beta-binomial) live in the stats module outside this scope, so the 'unguarded statsmodels import' concern does not apply to these files.
- FORMAT FORWARD-COMPAT for rubric.bundle is strong: canonical_bundle_json uses allow_nan=False (refuses NaN/Inf), version is deliberately excluded from the hash with a documented rationale, and assert_versioned is a genuine fail-closed andon for the §9.1 'no silent retconning' invariant (both retcon directions covered + tested). compile_bundle is a pure hash with no version surface to misparse.
- CALIBRATION LOADER tamper/corruption robustness is strong: structured coded errors on every malformed-input path (missing path, bad JSON, wrong shape, unknown category, unknown key, bad expected_pass type, bool-as-float, duplicate ids), each with a hint, each test-proven RED. No silent-accept paths found. (The only gap is the unbounded read in finding 005, a resource limit, not a correctness/tamper gap — there is no manifest-hash verification here because this loader has no manifest; split-manifest hashing lives in tuning.split_inventory and is sound.)
- NUMERICAL ROBUSTNESS in irt.point_biserial is exemplary: explicit guards for n<2, zero item variance, zero total variance, and a defensive post-corrcoef np.isnan check, all returning 0.0 (the correct 'no discrimination' signal) rather than NaN — every degenerate case tested. prune_items handles empty/ragged/no-item matrices with coded errors. The ONLY numerical gap in the whole domain is sobol_screen's NaN masking (001).
- known_groups handles missing/empty outcome maps as explicit violations (cannot accept an un-exercised instrument), infers tier rank defensively, and degrades unranked tiers to notes rather than crashing.

Severity calibration: no HIGH issued. Finding 001 is the most consequential (silent wrong screen result) but requires the operator's surrogate to emit a non-finite value and is caught downstream at the validation split, so MEDIUM. 002 is MEDIUM (future-proofing/dependency drift, realistic over the inspect-ai 0.x cadence). 003/004/005 are LOW (defensive/resource gaps on operator-controlled, not adversarial, surfaces).
